evidence_text: The study uses a 3 × 3 factorial design crossing three write strategies with three retrieval methods.
citation: Yuan et al., 2026, Abstract and Section 1
source_excerpt: “We conduct a controlled 3 × 3 factorial study that crosses three write strategies (raw chunks, Mem0-style extraction, MemGPT-style summarization) with three retrieval methods (cosine similarity, BM25, hybrid reranking).”
note:
This is a study-design fact, not a performance claim.
```
caveat:
This is a study-design fact, not a performance claim.
```

evidence_text: The evaluation uses LoCoMo with 1,540 non-adversarial questions.
citation: Yuan et al., 2026, Section 1 and Appendix B
source_excerpt: “Evaluating on LoCoMo (1,540 non-adversarial questions)” and “We evaluate on 1,540 non-adversarial questions.”
note:
The result scope is bounded to this benchmark slice.
```
caveat:
The result scope is bounded to this benchmark slice.
```

evidence_text: Average accuracy spans 57.1% to 77.2% across retrieval methods.
source_excerpt: “On LoCoMo, retrieval method is the dominant factor: average accuracy spans 20 points across retrieval methods (57.1% to 77.2%).”
note:
This is an across-retrieval-method average, not a single-configuration score.
```
caveat:
This is an across-retrieval-method average, not a single-configuration score.
```

evidence_text: Within a retrieval column, write strategy changes accuracy by only 3 to 8 points.
citation: Yuan et al., 2026, Abstract and Section 3.1
source_excerpt: “Accuracy varies by 20 points across retrieval methods but only 3 to 8 points across write strategies.” “Table 1 shows that write strategy accounts for only 3–8 accuracy points within any retrieval column.”
note:
The source frames this as much smaller than the retrieval-method effect.
```
caveat:
The source frames this as much smaller than the retrieval-method effect.
```

evidence_text: Basic RAG stores raw 3-turn conversation chunks with speaker names and timestamps and requires zero LLM calls at write time.
citation: Yuan et al., 2026, Section 2.1 and Appendix B
source_excerpt: “Basic RAG stores raw 3-turn conversation chunks with speaker names and timestamps, requiring zero LLM calls at write time.” Table 3 also lists “Write Basic RAG Store raw 3-turn chunks None.”
note:
This is the write-time storage and cost setup for one condition.
```
caveat:
This is the write-time storage and cost setup for one condition.
```

evidence_text: Basic RAG reaches 77.9% accuracy under cosine retrieval and 81.1% under hybrid retrieval.
citation: Yuan et al., 2026, Table 1 and Section 3.1
source_excerpt: Table 1 reports for “Basic RAG” accuracy of “77.9” under “Cos” and “81.1” under “Hyb.” Section 3.1 states, “Basic RAG, which stores raw chunks with zero LLM calls, achieves 77.9% under cosine and 81.1% under hybrid.”
note:
These are configuration-specific scores, not averages.
```
caveat:
These are configuration-specific scores, not averages.
```

evidence_text: The only reported retrieval-column exception is BM25, where Summarized Episodes scores 62.7% and Basic RAG scores 59.2%.
source_excerpt: “The one exception is BM25, where Summarized Episodes (62.7%) edges out Basic RAG (59.2%), likely because keyword retrieval benefits from the denser vocabulary of compressed summaries.”
note:
This is presented as an exception to the general pattern that raw chunking matches or exceeds the other write strategies; the causal explanation is stated as likely, not established.
```
caveat:
This is presented as an exception to the general pattern that raw chunking matches or exceeds the other write strategies; the causal explanation is stated as likely, not established.
```

evidence_text: Hybrid reranking averages 77.2% accuracy across write strategies, versus 73.4% for cosine and 57.1% for BM25.
citation: Yuan et al., 2026, Section 3.2 and Table 1
source_excerpt: “Hybrid reranking averages 77.2% across write strategies, compared to 73.4% for cosine and 57.1% for BM25.”
note:
These are averages over the three write strategies.
```
caveat:
These are averages over the three write strategies.
```

evidence_text: Retrieval precision and downstream accuracy are reported as near-perfectly correlated with r = 0.98.
source_excerpt: “Retrieval precision is near-perfectly correlated with downstream accuracy (r = 0.98, Figure 4), providing strong evidence that retrieval quality most strongly correlates with downstream performance.”
note:
This evidence is correlational; it supports retrieval quality as the stronger associated factor but does not by itself establish causation.
```
caveat:
This evidence is correlational; it supports retrieval quality as the stronger associated factor but does not by itself establish causation.
```

evidence_text: Retrieval failure is the dominant reported error mode and accounts for 11% to 46% of all questions depending on configuration.
source_excerpt: “Retrieval failure is the dominant error mode, accounting for 11–46% of all questions depending on configuration.”
note:
The reported range is across the nine evaluated configurations.
```
caveat:
The reported range is across the nine evaluated configurations.
```

evidence_text: Under BM25 with Extracted Facts, retrieval failure reaches 46.3% of all questions.
citation: Yuan et al., 2026, Section 3.3 and Table 2
source_excerpt: “Under BM25 with Extracted Facts, retrieval failure alone reaches 46.3%, nearly matching the total error rate.” Table 2 reports “Ext. Facts BM25 … Retrieval 46.3.”
note:
This is the highest retrieval-failure figure explicitly highlighted in the paper text.
```
caveat:
This is the highest retrieval-failure figure explicitly highlighted in the paper text.
```

evidence_text: For Basic RAG, hybrid reranking reduces retrieval failure from 35.3% under BM25 to 11.4%.
source_excerpt: “Hybrid reranking cuts retrieval failures roughly in half (e.g., 35.3% to 11.4% for Basic RAG).”
note:
This directly supports the claim that the hybrid retrieval procedure improves what gets surfaced at inference time.
```
caveat:
This directly supports the claim that the hybrid retrieval procedure improves what gets surfaced at inference time.
```

evidence_text: Across configurations, utilization failures stay between 4% and 8% and hallucinations stay between 0.4% and 1.4%.
citation: Yuan et al., 2026, Section 3.3 and Table 2
source_excerpt: “By contrast, utilization failures remain stable at 4–8% and hallucinations at 0.4–1.4% regardless of configuration.”
note:
The source presents these ranges as comparatively stable relative to retrieval failures.
```
caveat:
The source presents these ranges as comparatively stable relative to retrieval failures.
```

evidence_text: Under Basic RAG plus Hybrid, the utilization probe marks 79.0% of questions as Beneficial.
citation: Yuan et al., 2026, Section 3.3 and Table 2
source_excerpt: “The utilization probe reinforces this: beneficial rates reach 79.0% under Basic RAG + Hybrid, meaning the model improves its answer four out of five times when given good retrievals.” Table 2 reports “Basic RAG Hybrid … Beneficial 79.0.”
note:
This is a probe outcome for one configuration, not a global average.
```
caveat:
This is a probe outcome for one configuration, not a global average.
```

evidence_text: The authors state that the study’s generality is limited because it uses one backbone model, one benchmark, and a fixed retrieval budget of k = 5.
source_excerpt: “Our study evaluates a single backbone model (GPT-5-mini) on one benchmark (LoCoMo) with a fixed retrieval budget (k = 5), limiting generality.”
note:
This caveat bounds how far the reported findings can be generalized beyond the tested setup.
```
caveat:
This caveat bounds how far the reported findings can be generalized beyond the tested setup.
```

evidence_text: For answer-correctness validation, the LLM judge achieves 92% accuracy and Cohen’s kappa of 0.82 against human annotations.
source_excerpt: “The LLM judge achieves 92% accuracy with substantial agreement (Cohen’s κ = 0.82), validating its use for measuring answer accuracy in Table 1.”
note:
This validation result is for the answer-correctness judging setup.
```
caveat:
This validation result is for the answer-correctness judging setup.
```

evidence_text: For failure-mode classification on incorrect answers, the LLM judge achieves 85% accuracy, with most confusion between retrieval failure and utilization failure.
source_excerpt: “The LLM judge achieves 85% accuracy, with most confusion between retrieval failure and utilization failure. This reflects the inherent difficulty in distinguishing whether the system failed to retrieve the right information or failed to use correctly-retrieved information.”
note: This keeps a methodological caveat attached to the failure-mode evidence: the retrieval-versus-utilization boundary is described as inherently ambiguous even for human judges.
caveat: This keeps a methodological caveat attached to the failure-mode evidence: the retrieval-versus-utilization boundary is described as inherently ambiguous even for human judges.
